Selected Work

Case studies & teardowns

Each case follows the same arc: real user pain → segmentation → scored solutions → metrics.

📁 Adding new case studies: Upload your case PDF to the cases/ folder in your GitHub repo. Name it clearly (e.g. amazon-search-redesign.pdf). Then edit index.html — copy one of the existing case card blocks and update the title, summary, metrics, and PDF filename. The link format is: href="cases/your-file.pdf"
✓ Real case Ola MapsPlatform · 0→1
Designing India's map search experience to challenge Google Maps
Ola Maps was entering a market owned by Google Maps. Task: design a differentiated, India-first search experience — and drive adoption through Ola's 50M+ ride users, not a standalone launch.
3 competitors benchmarked across 11 feature dimensions
AR Live View RICE score: 92.57 — shipped first
3 India-first differentiators designed
Click to expand ↗ View full case ↗

Rather than trying to serve all use cases at once, I scoped to the Ola rides passenger — highest-reach, fastest-feedback cohort — and built outward. The core question: what can an India-first maps product do that Google can't or won't?

01Discovery
Benchmarked Google Maps, Apple Maps, and MapMyIndia across 11 features in 3 categories. Mapped 3 user types and identified "waiting for cab" and "in-ride" as zero-engagement windows — perfect surfaces to introduce Maps without friction.
02Prioritisation
Applied RICE scoring across 3 features. Interactive Map + AR Live View scored 92.57 — ahead of Social Connect (42) and Virtual Preview (72). Key differentiator: competitors only offer AR for pedestrians. Ola could offer it to passengers in-ride.
03Delivery
Designed AR Live View — passengers point camera, get real-time category search overlaid on feed. Restricted to passengers only to prevent driver distraction. Wireframed Before Trip, During Trip, and AR Live View states.
92.57
RICE score — AR Live View (top-ranked feature)
11
Feature dimensions benchmarked across 3 competitors
3
Differentiated India-first features designed
Key learning
When entering a winner-takes-all market, the fastest path to awareness is riding the distribution you already own. The Ola rides app was the trojan horse. 50M users who already trust Ola for navigation beat any marketing budget.
✓ Real case SwiggyConsumer · 0→1 Feature
Swiggy Regular — solving decision fatigue for India's daily food orderers
India's working class orders food 5–6× a week but burns 50% of ordering time just deciding what to eat. Root cause was paradox of choice — not price, not delivery speed.
Swiggy Regular: RICE 74.67 — highest of 3 solutions
20% time saved per order
North Star: Revenue from scheduled orders
Click to expand ↗ View full case ↗

Investor pitch case for Swiggy. Mapped the 7-step ordering flow with psychological load scores at each step, found restaurant + item selection caused the most cognitive friction, then RICE-scored three competing solutions.

01Discovery
Segmented India into 5 age cohorts by food ordering intensity. Prioritised independent working class (23–30). Mapped 7-step flow with psych load scores. Steps 3+4 (restaurant + item) = 50% of time, highest negative psych score.
02Prioritisation
JTBD: "When I need to order regularly but don't want decision overhead every time, help me avoid repetition." RICE: Swiggy Regular (74.67) vs Surprise Me! (45) vs Tiffin partnership (40.5). Regular won on balanced effort-reach.
03Delivery
Designed Swiggy Regular as a new home section. Users set week-long schedule — restaurant + item + time per day. Advance payment via wallet. 5–7 day threshold for discounted rates. Repeat cycle checkbox. Full wireframes built.
74.67
RICE score — highest of 3 competing solutions
20%
Time saved per fulfilled order
5
Metrics categories: Discovery, Adoption, Engagement, Revenue, Retention
Key learning
The biggest friction wasn't price or speed — it was the decision itself. Paradox of choice is real and measurable. The solution that removes cognitive load, not just clicks, wins.

Teardowns

Products I'd fix

Structured critique: what works, what doesn't, what I'd change — and why.

Teardown #1 — Zepto (Quick Commerce)
Why 10-minute delivery has a trust problem
✓ What works
  • Live order tracking — every state visible
  • Dark store density creates speed moat
  • Category UI is fast and scannable
✗ What doesn't
  • Substitutions happen silently — discovered at doorstep
  • Refund flow: 5 taps + chat agent
  • No freshness signal for produce
→ What I'd change
  • Pre-notify substitutions before dispatch, 1-tap reject
  • Self-serve refund under ₹200 — no agent
  • "Packed today" badge on perishables
Verdict: Speed is solved. The next moat is trust. Post-order experience is where repeat purchase is won or lost.
Teardown #2 — Notion (Productivity)
The blank page problem no one has fixed
✓ What works
  • Block-based editing — flexible without complexity
  • Database views are genuinely powerful
  • Template gallery sets expectations
✗ What doesn't
  • Blank canvas kills new user activation
  • Search is slow, returns stale results
  • Mobile is a port, not a rethink
→ What I'd change
  • Role-based onboarding → pre-fill relevant workspace
  • Persistent search index
  • Mobile-first edit mode, simplified blocks
Verdict: Notion's TAM is limited by onboarding, not features. The blank-page fix is worth more than any new block.
Teardown #3 — LinkedIn Job Applications
Great for everyone except the person applying
✓ What works
  • Easy Apply reduces friction to near zero
  • Network + discovery in one feed
  • Recruiter signals set some expectations
✗ What doesn't
  • Applications disappear — no status, no timeline
  • No salary, vague requirements
  • Ghosting is systemic; volume beats quality
→ What I'd change
  • Application status pipeline per job
  • Mandatory salary range on all postings
  • Auto-close stale applications at 30 days
Verdict: LinkedIn optimises for recruiters. The product that serves applicants will own the next decade of talent markets.

Work Experience

  • Reduced stockouts 40% and improved festive fulfilment efficiency 25% via demand-velocity modelling and capacity-aware delivery slot logic
  • Unlocked corporate gifting workflows — contributing 9% of festive-season orders at 4.2× AOV vs regular purchases
  • Increased Satya Retail partner adoption 32% by rebuilding FoS workflows and promotional tooling, improving operational productivity 24%

About

Why I do this

MK

I started as a software engineer building blockchain infrastructure at 19,000 transactions per second. Then I realised I cared more about why we were building than how — and I went back to get an MBA from MDI Gurgaon (CAT 98.53 percentile) specifically to make that switch with credibility.

At PwC, I've been the embedded PM for India's premium D2C grocery brands — not an advisor, an owner. When checkout conversion dropped, I fixed it. When nobody knew why 47% of users were dropping off, I built the server-side GA4 instrumentation that surfaced it. When a corporate gifting segment didn't exist, I built the workflows that made it 9% of festive revenue at 4.2× average order value.

What makes me different: I have a technical foundation that lets me talk to engineers without a translator, an analytics instinct that defaults to data before opinion, and enough structured problem-solving practice (CAT top 1.5%, SAFe POPM, CSM) to stay calm when things are messy. Most PMs can write a PRD. Fewer can write SQL to validate whether it worked.

I'm currently open to Senior PM roles in consumer tech, platform products, or AI-driven workflows — based in Gurgaon, open to Bengaluru or remote.
